---
title: "6 · Forecast of the soil state"
---

The same closed water budget that produces the hindcast state ([Physics — hydrology](03a-physics-hydrology.qmd))
can be driven **forward** to produce the *antecedent* hydrologic state a hazard is about to encounter.
The forecast product is the antecedent state — water table, soil moisture, saturation — not the flood
hydrograph itself. This work is tracked as epic #73 (issues #70–#72).

## Precipitation forcing (source-agnostic)

The water budget's precipitation input is deliberately not tied to any one product. It accepts observed
precipitation for hindcasts and forecast precipitation for the forward run, each downscaled to the
90 m grid and bias-aware. Every downscaled field records its native versus delivered resolution, so a
coarse forecast pixel is never read as a native observation.

## Hindcast from observed precipitation (PRISM preferred)

For hindcasts, the preferred rainfall source is **PRISM** — gauge-anchored and best-resolved for the
PNW — driving the budget over a documented event to produce the GWL/θ/saturation/recharge history
(#70). A reanalysis such as TerraClimate can be substituted; **ERA5-Land** is reserved as an
*independent evaluation* dataset, not a forcing (#72).

## Forward forcing from AI weather forecasts + downscaling

For the forward ensemble, quantitative precipitation forecasts from **GraphCast** and/or **ACE2** are
ingested via [earth2studio](https://github.com/NVIDIA/earth2studio) (#71). The ~25 km forecast
precipitation is **downscaled to 90 m** with orographic physics — bilinear resampling loses the
Cascade atmospheric-river enhancement — and bias-corrected against the PRISM climatology. Running the
budget forward over the ensemble yields a forecast antecedent state with spread, handed off through the
dynamic export ([Hazard integration](07-hazard-integration.qmd)).

## Antecedent state, not hydrograph — scope and caveats

The forecast forces the *subsurface antecedent state*, not the surface flood response: converting
recharge and runoff into a flood hydrograph requires a separate surface-routing model (#55). The value
here is that a saturated antecedent state — a water table already raised by a winter AR sequence — is
what turns the next storm into a landslide or a liquefaction-susceptible condition, and that is exactly
the state the twin forecasts.
